AI.method: The paper employs a large language model for automated mapping of model instances to target metamodels and merging of metamodels. These transformations are supported by structural validation of generated models for accuracy.
AI.result: The proposed LLM-driven approach demonstrated feasibility in handling transformations using Ecore and SysML v2 metamodels. Case studies in the automotive domain confirmed its ability to reduce manual effort and produce structurally valid models.
AI.conclusion: Large language models provide an effective mechanism for solving interoperability challenges in Model-Driven Engineering, reducing manual transformation efforts while improving model accuracy across heterogeneous tools.

title: The Steering Budget: Examples beat Knobs
summary:
Generative models are steered with knobs -- prompts, guidance scales, property tags. Turn one as hard as you like and, past a point, it stops moving the property you care about. We find that ceiling is not a shortcoming of the model but a budget, set by the training data before the model is trained: a property's movable range splits in two -- the part a knob can reach, and a second, significant part that only examples -- concrete instances of what you want more of -- can reach. That second part is usually much larger, but not always, and the same budget says so in advance.
  Reaching that second part takes a different move: instead of turning a knob, you show the model examples, composed from what it already learned rather than added to its training. A cheap audit of the training data measures the budget; we give a recipe for building the example set that reaches all of it.
  This buys two things a knob can't. Reach: it moves a property across the whole budget, not just the part a knob reaches. Expressiveness: it steers toward targets you can only specify by example -- including ones you can't put into words. We turn these into a handful of falsifiable claims and verify them in two unrelated domains, image and crystal-structure generation -- marking where a knob is enough, and where only examples will do.

AI.tldr: Generative models have intrinsic limits in property modification using prompts and knobs, defined by training data. A significant portion of adjustments can only be achieved through examples, not knobs.
AI.motivation: To explore and address the limitations of steering generative models with knobs and prompts, and identify alternative ways to extend their functionality.
AI.method: The authors conducted analyses on training data's impact on property adjustability, proposed a method that utilizes examples instead of knobs for steering, and tested their claims in image and crystal-structure generation.
AI.result: Using examples allowed the model to reach properties unattainable by knobs alone and enabled specification of properties that can't be articulated in words.
AI.conclusion: Generative models' property steering has a ceiling defined by training data. Leveraging examples expands the adjustable range and expressiveness beyond the reach of traditional knob-based methods.

title: Heterogeneous Element-Aware Cross-Version Differencing of Scientific Documents via Layout-Aware Alignment and Structure-Aware Reasoning
summary: Cross-version differencing of scientific documents is essential in scholarly publishing and technical documentation, but remains challenging because scientific documents are page-structured artifacts containing heterogeneous elements such as text, tables, formulas, figures, and layout cues. Existing text-sequence-based methods often lose layout and structural information, while image-based methods lack semantic interpretability and are sensitive to rendering variation. To address these limitations, this paper proposes a layout-aware heterogeneous element-aware framework for scientific document differencing. The framework decomposes document versions into semantically typed elements, establishes cross-version correspondence through an alignment-first mechanism that jointly models spatial, content, and structural compatibility, and performs type-aware difference reasoning over aligned element pairs. It supports unified change detection, localization, structure-awareness analysis, and alignment/matching evaluation across text, tables, formulas, and figures. Experiments on real-world scientific PDF data from journal production proofreading workflows show that the proposed framework consistently outperforms element-specific baselines. It achieves detection F1 scores of 0.903, 0.855, 0.862, and 0.845 for text, tables, formulas, and figures, respectively, with further improvements in localization, structure awareness, and matching quality. Ablation and sensitivity analyses confirm the effectiveness of cross-version alignment, type-specific representations, structure-aware reasoning, and compatibility-weight design. These results demonstrate that heterogeneous element-aware differencing provides a robust and interpretable solution for scientific document comparison in realistic editorial production scenarios.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces a framework for comparing different versions of scientific documents, addressing challenges posed by heterogeneous components like text, tables, and figures.
AI.motivation: Existing methods lose layout and semantic details or are sensitive to rendering issues, creating a need for better differencing methods for scientific documents.
AI.method: The framework combines element decomposition, cross-version alignment modeling spatial, content, and structural compatibility, and type-specific difference reasoning for unified analysis.
AI.result: The framework achieves high detection F1 scores (0.903 for text, 0.855 for tables, 0.862 for formulas, and 0.845 for figures) and improves change localization, structure awareness, and element matching.
AI.conclusion: The proposed framework is effective for robust and interpretable scientific document comparison in editorial workflows.

title: Beyond Implicit Force: Evaluating Explicit Force-Torque Proxies in Action Chunking with Transformers
summary: Contact-rich manipulation requires policies to infer interaction state from signals that are often weakly observable through vision and kinematics alone. Action Chunking with Transformers (ACT) has shown strong performance in fine-grained manipulation, but many deployments collect demonstrations through leader-follower teleoperation, where tracking error between commanded leader motion and executed follower motion implicitly encodes contact, resistance, and constraint violation. This paper examines whether ACT's apparent force-awareness depends on this hidden interaction cue. We introduce an observation-centric ACT variant that predicts future follower joint states instead of leader commands, thereby removing the teleoperation-induced discrepancy signal while preserving the rest of the learning pipeline. We then evaluate whether simple joint-torque proxies, derived from onboard motor current or joint effort, can recover contact-aware behavior without external force/torque sensors. Across four real-world tasks spanning surface following, insertion, stiffness discrimination, and force-based stopping, removing the implicit cue leads to severe failures in force-critical phases. In contrast, torque-augmented policies recover robust contact behavior and improve the base ACT policy. These results demonstrate that, on real hardware, the implicit teleoperation cue is a recoverable source of force-awareness, where torque signals are available, a simple proxy matches, surpasses, or further enhances it.

AI.tldr: The paper explores whether implicit force-awareness in ACT model for contact-rich manipulation arises from teleoperation cues and investigates whether torque signals can replace such cues.
AI.motivation: To understand if ACT's success in contact-rich tasks derives from hidden cues during teleoperation, and whether simpler signals like joint-torque can replicate or improve force-aware performance.
AI.method: An observation-centric ACT variant was introduced to remove implicit teleoperation cues and experiments were conducted with joint-torque proxies on real-world tasks.
AI.result: Results showed failure in force-critical phases without teleoperation cues, while torque-augmented policies recovered robust contact behaviors and improved on ACT.
AI.conclusion: Implicit teleoperation cues are crucial for force-awareness in ACT, but simpler torque proxies can substitute and outperform them.

title: Reinforcing Egocentric Spatial Perception in Multimodal Large Language Models via Ego Scene Augmentation
comment: 14 pages, 8 figures. Chi Kit Wong and Ye Pan contributed equally. Code: https://github.com/Chikit-WONG/spatialGraph
summary: Egocentric Visual Question Answering (VQA) has attracted widespread attention as an important task for enabling Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) to interact with the real world. However, existing MLLMs struggle to perform effective spatial reasoning in complex egocentric scenes due to their limited spatial perception capabilities. To this end, we introduce Ego Scene Augmentation (ESA), an egocentric spatial perception framework, which actively enhances the spatial perception capabilities from the egocentric perspective, powered by the proposed Ego-element Graph. Our core insight is leveraging the Ego-element Graph as an intermediary representation to augment the egocentric spatial perception of MLLMs via visual foundational models. Specifically, we 1) construct the Ego-element Graph, which encapsulates and integrates egocentric spatial features enabled by visual foundational models; 2) enhance the spatial perception capabilities of MLLMs via the Ego-element Graph for ego-perspective scenes. Our proposed ESA framework presents significant performance improvement on the EgoTextVQA benchmark. We achieve an 8.14% gain on the indoor setting and an 8.72% gain on the outdoor setting. Furthermore, our ESA shows the most impressive performance improvement in the shopping subset of the indoor setting. The project code is publicly available.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces Ego Scene Augmentation (ESA), a framework aimed at improving egocentric spatial perception in Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs). ESA leverages an Ego-element Graph to enhance spatial reasoning capabilities in complex egocentric environments, achieving notable performance improvements on the EgoTextVQA benchmark.
AI.motivation: Existing MLLMs struggle with spatial reasoning in complex egocentric visual scenes due to limited spatial perception capabilities, necessitating advancements for better real-world interaction.
AI.method: ESA constructs an Ego-element Graph that captures egocentric spatial features using visual foundational models, and employs this graph to enhance the spatial reasoning abilities of MLLMs.
AI.result: ESA demonstrated significant improvements in performance on the EgoTextVQA benchmark, with gains of 8.14% in indoor settings and 8.72% in outdoor settings, particularly excelling in shopping scenarios.
AI.conclusion: The proposed ESA framework effectively enhances egocentric spatial perception in MLLMs, providing a strong performance boost in challenging visual scenarios. The project is open-source.

title: Post Hoc Inference for Component Attribution in Multivariate Change-Point Detection
summary: We consider the post-detection analysis of change-points for multivariate time series, with the goal of identifying which coordinates are responsible for a detected change. After a change-point has been located by an offline detection algorithm, we propose post hoc statistical procedures to determine whether the change occurs in either of two predefined blocks of coordinates or in both. Our methods rely on two-sample testing procedures with a particular focus on nonparametric tests; we provide theoretical guarantees for Type I error control. Simulations and a real-data experiment demonstrate the strong performance of the proposed procedures.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a post-detection analysis method for multivariate time series, focusing on pinpointing the responsible coordinates for change-points using statistical techniques.
AI.motivation: Address the challenge of identifying the specific coordinates responsible for changes after detecting a change-point in multivariate time series.
AI.method: Post hoc statistical procedures leveraging two-sample testing techniques, especially nonparametric tests, with theoretical guarantees on Type I error control.
AI.result: Simulations and real-data experiments validate the effectiveness of the proposed procedures.
AI.conclusion: The proposed methods provide reliable statistical tools to identify the sources of change in multivariate time series, maintaining control over Type I errors.

title: Local Additive Feature Attribution: A Mathematical Taxonomy and Reporting Checklist
comment: 35 pages, 7 figures, and 19 tables. Ancillary files include the axiom matrix, reporting checklist, related-survey scoring, and review-corpus summary
summary: Feature-attribution methods are central to explainable artificial intelligence. Their assumptions are expressed in several mathematical languages: cooperative-game values, path integrals, gradient operators, perturbation distributions, and backpropagation rules. This survey proposes a common framework for local additive feature attribution. It organizes Shapley, path-based, gradient/backpropagation, perturbation, and CAM-style methods around five specification choices: value function, reference, path, perturbation distribution, and conservation rule. It then compares these methods through an axiom-by-method matrix and links common failure modes, including baseline sensitivity, off-manifold perturbations, sanity-check failures, adversarial manipulation, and method disagreement, to the assumptions that produce them. Finally, the survey proposes a ten-item reporting checklist for studies that use local additive attributions. The central message is that attribution results are meaningful only relative to the mathematical assumptions under which they are defined, and that those assumptions should be reported.

AI.tldr: The paper surveys feature-attribution methods in explainable AI and organizes them around a unified framework, emphasizing the importance of reporting mathematical assumptions.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to clarify and unify existing feature-attribution methods in explainable AI, improve their understanding, and address common issues through a consistent framework.
AI.method: The authors propose a framework for local additive feature attribution, organize existing methods around five key specification choices, and compare them using an axiom-based analysis.
AI.result: The survey highlights common failure modes in attribution methods, links them to their underlying assumptions, and provides a ten-item checklist for proper reporting of assumptions.
AI.conclusion: Feature-attribution methods should always report their underlying mathematical assumptions as attribution results are meaningful only in the context of those assumptions.

title: Large Language Models for Code Generation from Multilingual Prompts: A Curated Benchmark and a Study on Code Quality
summary: Large Language Models (LLMs) perform differently on identical programming tasks when prompted in different natural languages, a phenomenon known as language bias. While this behavior has been widely studied for general text generation, its impact on code generation quality and programming conventions remains largely unexplored. We investigate how the language used to describe programming tasks affects the source code generated by GPT-4o mini, DeepSeek, and Claude. Our study comprises 460 coding tasks spanning Python (230) and Java (230). We translate and manually curate the original English prompts into Chinese, Hindi, Spanish, and Italian while preserving their technical meaning. We evaluate the generated code using multiple dimensions, including functional correctness through test pass rates, structural quality using established code metrics, issues detected by static analysis tools, and lexical characteristics such as the language used in identifiers and comments. Our results show that (i) English prompts do not consistently produce the best functional correctness or code quality, (ii) the impact of prompt language depends on both the programming language and the LLM, and (iii) generated code frequently mixes English with the prompt language in comments and string literals. These findings provide the first curated multilingual benchmark for studying language bias in code generation and offer insights for developing more robust multilingual code generation systems.

AI.tldr: The paper studies how the natural language of prompts affects code generation quality in LLMs through multilingual experiments, revealing language biases and offering a benchmark.
AI.motivation: Language biases in Large Language Models (LLMs) for text generation are known, but their impact on code generation, in terms of functional correctness, structural quality, and programming conventions, remains underexplored.
AI.method: The authors evaluated GPT-4o mini, DeepSeek, and Claude on 460 coding tasks in Python and Java using prompts translated into Chinese, Hindi, Spanish, Italian, and English. They assessed functional correctness, code structure, static analysis issues, and lexical characteristics.
AI.result: The results show that English prompts do not consistently yield the best code, the effect of prompt language is influenced by the programming language and the LLM, and generated code often includes multilingual elements.
AI.conclusion: The study provides a multilingual benchmark and insights to mitigate language bias in LLMs for code generation, promoting robustness in multilingual programming scenarios.

title: Automatic Hard Example Synthesis with Multi-Level Agentic Data Curation
summary: Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) are increasingly deployed for nuanced content safety and moderation tasks, yet they remain vulnerable to adversarial attacks and out-of-distribution edge cases. Traditional active learning and manual annotation fail to scale against the complexity and volume of novel multimodal threats. In this paper, we propose an automated, agentic red-teaming framework that systematically synthesizes difficult examples using an iterative strategy that proposes novel hypotheses as well as mutating on past attempts. Leveraging a multi-agent architecture that consists of a high-reasoning Architect agent, an advanced image generator, and a multi-level verification committee of LLM raters, our system autonomously uncovers boundary-pushing violations and ambiguous policy edge cases without any human intervention. By employing these carefully synthesized adversarial examples as in-context demonstrations via test-time Retrieval, we substantially improve the target model's robustness, reducing the False Negative Rate (FNR) from 41.2% to 24.5% in a public image safety benchmark without relying on any human labeling.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces an automated red-teaming framework to enhance the robustness of multimodal large language models (MLLMs) for content safety without requiring human labeling. This reduces the False Negative Rate significantly.
AI.motivation: Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) face vulnerabilities in content safety tasks due to adversarial attacks, emerging multimodal threats, and their complexity, which active learning and manual annotation cannot effectively handle at scale.
AI.method: The proposed automated framework uses a multi-agent system with an Architect agent for reasoning, an advanced image generator, and LLM raters for verification. It iteratively generates and mutates examples to uncover adversarial and ambiguous cases autonomously.
AI.result: The method demonstrates its effectiveness by reducing the False Negative Rate (FNR) from 41.2% to 24.5% on a public image safety benchmark, using synthesized examples for in-context learning without manual annotation.
AI.conclusion: The framework improves the robustness of MLLMs in content safety tasks, offering a scalable and human-independent approach for identifying policy edge cases and adversarial examples.

title: Budgeted Subset Refinement for Execution-Aware LLM Research Ideation
summary: Large language models (LLMs) can generate research ideas that appear novel to expert reviewers, but recent work also shows that such ideas often lack diversity, are difficult for LLMs to evaluate reliably, and may fail to translate into strong executed projects. This paper evaluates a controlled proxy benchmark for a pre-execution scaffolding problem: given a noisy pool of LLM-generated research ideas, how should a system allocate limited refinement effort to construct a stronger, more diverse, more execution-aware portfolio for human researchers under a fixed rubric? We introduce Budgeted Subset Refinement, a family of strategies that refine only a selected subset of candidates rather than refining all candidates uniformly. In a unified shared-candidate-pool evaluation across 10 random seeds and 10 research-ideation environments, raw generation and reranking alone produce no research-strong nonduplicate ideas under the benchmark rubric, while refinement is necessary for strong proxy-rated portfolios. Uniform refinement produces strong individual ideas but is not the best portfolio-level allocation of compute. Random-k refinement is a strong low-cost baseline, while diversity-aware MMR-k refinement gives the best overall proxy tradeoff: the highest research-strong nonduplicate yield, the lowest duplicate rate among successful methods, and the best cost per research-strong nonduplicate idea. A blinded external-judge robustness check on a balanced 72-item sample supports the broad refinement effect across independent model families, while showing that per-item rankings among refined strategies vary by judge. These results suggest that LLM research ideation systems should be evaluated not only as idea generators, but as budgeted support-allocation systems. The claims are scoped to proxy-rated portfolio quality and do not substitute for expert review or execution-grounded validation.

AI.tldr: This paper explores strategies for refining LLM-generated research ideas to improve portfolio quality under a fixed resource budget, emphasizing diversity, execution-awareness, and cost-efficiency.
AI.motivation: There is a need to overcome limitations of raw LLM-generated research ideas, which often lack diversity, evaluation reliability, and feasibility for execution.
AI.method: The paper proposes Budgeted Subset Refinement strategies, refining selected subsets of noisy LLM-generated ideas, rather than uniformly refining all ideas, for better portfolio outcomes.
AI.result: Refinement improves portfolio quality; uniform refinement boosts individual idea quality but isn't optimal for portfolios. Diversity-aware methods like MMR-k refinement balance diversity, execution-awareness, and cost for superior outcomes.
AI.conclusion: LLM research ideation systems should be seen as budgeted support allocation systems, and refinement strategies are crucial for strong portfolio-level performance. Expert review and execution validation are still required.

title: SoftNav: Injecting 3D Scene Tokens into VLMs for Embodied Navigation
comment: 8 pages, 6 figures. Accepted to IEEE/RSJ International Conference on Intelligent Robots and Systems (IROS) 2026
summary: In goal-directed embodied navigation, where an agent must locate a specified target in an unseen environment, 3D scene understanding and navigation reasoning must work in concert. Current approaches transmit 3D scene information to vision-language models (VLMs) through text, suggesting a representation gap in our tested configurations; a controlled ablation confirms that direct embedding-level transfer significantly outperforms the evaluated text serialization formats. We introduce SoftNav, which injects entity-level 3D continuous representations -- one token per detected object or frontier -- into a VLM's hidden space as soft tokens through a lightweight projector. With the 3D encoder and VLM frozen, only ~1,200 samples and ~17M trainable parameters are needed. On HM3D-OVON, SoftNav achieves 74.2%/68.3%/66.7% SR across three splits, surpassing all prior methods in both SR and SPL; the same navigation policy transfers zero-shot to GOAT-Bench (67.2% SR), SG3D (47.2% s-SR), and real-world robot deployment without retraining or architectural modification. Injecting 3D scene tokens directly into VLMs bridges the representation gap, enabling transferable navigation with minimal training.

AI.tldr: SoftNav integrates 3D continuous entity-level representations directly into vision-language model (VLM) hidden spaces as soft tokens, improving goal-directed embodied navigation.
AI.motivation: Current goal-directed navigation methods struggle with a representation gap when transmitting 3D scene data to vision-language models via text serialization formats.
AI.method: SoftNav utilizes a lightweight projector to inject detected object or frontier-level 3D representations as soft tokens into frozen VLMs, requiring minimal training data and parameters.
AI.result: SoftNav achieves superior success rates (SR) across multiple benchmarks such as HM3D-OVON, GOAT-Bench, SG3D, and even real-world robot deployment without retraining or architecture modification.
AI.conclusion: Direct embedding-level transfer of 3D scene data into VLMs bridges the representation gap, enabling high-performance navigation and zero-shot transferability with minimal resources.

title: Multi-Scale ViT Inference with Habitat-Fit Priors and kNN Retrieval for Multi-Species Plant Identification
summary: This paper describes DS@GT ARC's third-place solution to the PlantCLEF 2026 challenge on multi-species plant identification in vegetation quadrat images, where systems must predict every species present in high-resolution (~3000 x 3000 pixel) plot photographs while training only on single-label images of individual plants. The pipeline is built around a fine-tuned DINOv2 ViT-L/14 classifier applied over a multi-scale tile decomposition of each quadrat, with per-tile predictions blended with a FAISS kNN retriever and post-processed by source-aware temporal fusion across repeated plot visits, a habitat-fit demotion that injects geographic and altitude priors from the training data, and a South-Western Europe geographic mask. Habitat-fit demotion and multi-scale aggregation are the largest individual contributors in the ablations. Two complementary training-centric directions, a cross-region transformer with noisy-student distillation on the LUCAS dataset and a label-as-query transformer decoder over synthetic CLS-domain pseudo-quadrats, yielded null results. An inference-time augmentation with instance-aware segmentation crops also did not improve performance. The selected submission reaches a private-leaderboard macro-F1 of 0.43902 (third place; public 0.51096); an unselected configuration of the same pipeline scored above 0.45 on the private set. Code: https://github.com/dsgt-arc/plantclef-2026.

AI.tldr: The paper discusses DS@GT ARC's approach to the PlantCLEF 2026 challenge, achieving third place by identifying plant species from quadrat images using a hybrid pipeline combining a fine-tuned DINOv2 ViT-L/14 classifier, FAISS kNN retriever, and geographic-temporal post-processing.
AI.motivation: To address the challenging problem of identifying multiple plant species in high-resolution quadrat images, using only single-label training data from individual plants.
AI.method: The pipeline involves fine-tuning a DINOv2 ViT-L/14 classifier for multi-scale tile analysis, blending predictions with FAISS kNN retriever, and incorporating geographic and temporal post-processing techniques such as habitat-fit demotion and a South-Western Europe mask.
AI.result: The best solution achieved a private-leaderboard macro-F1 score of 0.43902, earning third place, with other pipeline configurations showing comparable or slightly improved performance.
AI.conclusion: The research highlights the effectiveness of geographic-temporal fusion and habitat-fit demotion, while demonstrating the challenges of training-centric innovations and inference-time segmentation in multi-species identification.

title: Tamed Stochastic Gradient Hamiltonian Monte Carlo
summary: In this paper, we propose a novel tamed stochastic gradient Hamiltonian Monte Carlo (tSGHMC) algorithm for sampling and stochastic optimization problems with superlinearly growing stochastic gradients. Under a certain continuity in average condition and a strong convexity condition, we establish a non-asymptotic error bound in Wasserstein-2 distance for tSGHMC with the rate of convergence equal to $1/4$. Then, we derive an upper estimate for the associated expected excess risk, which provides a theoretical guarantee for the performance of tSGHMC. To illustrate the effectiveness of the proposed algorithm, we apply tSGHMC to practical examples, including a newsvendor problem and a Conditional Value-at-Risk minimization problem, using synthetic and real-world datasets. Numerical results support our theoretical findings. Furthermore, we compare tSGHMC with its first-order counterpart, namely, the tamed unadjusted stochastic Langevin algorithm. Simulation results demonstrate that tSGHMC achieves lower root mean square error and expected excess risk across a range of tasks.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces the tamed stochastic gradient Hamiltonian Monte Carlo (tSGHMC) algorithm for optimizing and sampling problems with superlinearly growing stochastic gradients. It demonstrates superior performance through theoretical analysis and practical examples.
AI.motivation: Address the challenges posed by superlinearly growing stochastic gradients in sampling and optimization tasks while providing theoretical guarantees for improved performance.
AI.method: Develop a tamed variant of the stochastic gradient Hamiltonian Monte Carlo (tSGHMC) algorithm and establish its theoretical convergence properties. Apply it to real-world and synthetic datasets to validate its efficacy.
AI.result: tSGHMC achieves a convergence rate of 1/4 in Wasserstein-2 distance and offers better performance compared to first-order counterparts in numerical experiments.
AI.conclusion: tSGHMC is an effective and theoretically sound algorithm with significant advantages in handling superlinearly growing stochastic gradients, as evidenced by both theoretical analysis and practical results.

title: Lyapunov Guidance: A Unified Framework for Stabilizing Generative Flows
summary: Flow matching has emerged as an effective framework for learning complex data distributions, but adapting pretrained flow models to new tasks often requires computationally expensive retraining. Post-training guidance provides a more efficient alternative, but existing methods are largely heuristic and offer no explicit stability guarantees. We address this limitation by proposing LyaGuide, a unified Lyapunov-guided framework that formulates flow guidance as a Lyapunov control problem. Our main theoretical result establishes an equivalence between guided flow matching and Lyapunov control, thereby unifying common guidance strategies, such as classifier guidance, reward guidance, and energy-based guidance, within a single control-theoretic framework. To enforce the Lyapunov condition, we introduce a pseudo-projection operator with a closed-form expression that endows learned or heuristic guidance terms with explicit stability guarantees. LyaGuide supports two practical settings: a model-driven setting, where the target guidance distribution is specified through a known Lyapunov function, and a data-driven setting, where the guidance is adapted from task-specific downstream data. LyaGuide is compatible with existing guidance methods, introduces minimal additional computational overhead, and is straightforward to integrate in practice. Extensive experiments on synthetic benchmarks, image inverse problems, reinforcement learning planning, and energy-based modeling demonstrate consistent improvements in sample quality, guidance fidelity, and robustness, while maintaining computational efficiency.

AI.tldr: LyaGuide introduces a Lyapunov-guided framework for flow matching, improving guidance stability and efficiency in adapting pretrained models for various tasks.
AI.motivation: To address computational inefficiencies and lack of stability guarantees in post-training guidance methods for pretrained flow models.
AI.method: Proposes LyaGuide, a Lyapunov-guided flow matching framework with a theoretical foundation, supported by a pseudo-projection operator to ensure stability and compatibility with existing guidance strategies.
AI.result: LyaGuide achieves consistent improvements in sample quality, guidance fidelity, robustness, and computational efficiency across various applications.
AI.conclusion: LyaGuide is an effective and computationally efficient framework for guiding pretrained flow models with stability guarantees, adaptable to practical scenarios.

title: AI Prototyper: A Figma Plugin for Decomposition-Based GUI Prototyping with LLMs
summary: Graphical user interface (GUI) prototyping remains a time-consuming activity that demands both design expertise and considerable manual effort. As GUI prototypes are non-code artifacts that evolve alongside requirements throughout the development cycle, automating their generation is directly relevant to software maintenance and evolution. We present AI Prototyper, an open-source Figma plugin that automates GUI prototyping through a decomposition and retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) pipeline. Given a natural-language description of a desired screen, such as a login page or a product detail card, the plugin decomposes the request into discrete GUI features, retrieves matching components from a custom 32-primitive library, and renders each component as a fully editable Figma layer with auto-layout. The pipeline uses Gemini 2.5 Flash as its LLM back-end and a Node.js/Express service. Unlike existing decomposition-based tools, AI Prototyper introduces a human-in-the-loop editing step that lets users review, modify, or extend the generated feature list before rendering, uses a different technology stack and LLM family, and supports multilingual input, producing correctly labelled interfaces in Thai, English, and Mandarin Chinese. In a preliminary evaluation, participants using AI Prototyper completed more prototypes in a fixed time window than those working manually, and expert practitioners rated the AI-generated prototypes higher across nine quality dimensions. A demonstration video is available at https://youtu.be/pRoFAH7MQaE. The source code and component library are available at https://github.com/tongsalangsingha/AI-prototyper-tool

AI.tldr: AI Prototyper is an open-source Figma plugin that automates GUI prototyping using natural language descriptions, a custom library, and multilingual support, enhancing efficiency and prototype quality.
AI.motivation: The paper addresses the time-consuming and expertise-requiring nature of GUI prototyping, aiming to simplify and automate the process for enhanced software maintenance and evolution.
AI.method: AI Prototyper leverages a decomposition and retrieval-augmented generation pipeline, incorporating an LLM (Gemini 2.5 Flash), a human-in-the-loop editing step, and a unique 32-primitive library for GUI components.
AI.result: Participants using AI Prototyper created more prototypes in less time, and expert evaluations showed higher quality ratings for AI-generated prototypes across multiple dimensions.
AI.conclusion: AI Prototyper successfully automates GUI prototyping, improves productivity and design quality, and supports multilingual input, making it a significant advancement in GUI design automation.

title: AI Agents Do Not Fail Alone:The Context Fails First
summary: Context engineering has become central to building reliable AI agents, yet it remains largely unmeasured. Agents do not fail in isolation: their behavior is shaped by the instructions, tools, memory, retrieved knowledge, guardrails, and untrusted inputs accumulated in their context. When this context is weak, agents drift, hallucinate, misuse tools, ignore constraints, become vulnerable to injection, and waste tokens. This paper validates context-engineering quality as an independent leading indicator of agent reliability. We implement the measurement in ProofAgent-Harness, an open-source infrastructure for AI agent evaluation that uses multi-juror, consensus-based scoring. The harness assesses context across seven criteria: role clarity, guardrail coverage, instruction consistency, tool schema quality, grounding sufficiency, injection hardening, and token efficiency. Crucially, the context score is isolated from behavioral metrics and release decisions, enabling a non-circular validation. Through a controlled context-quality study across regulated agent domains, holding frontier LLM agents fixed and varying only their operating context, we show that context-quality criteria consistently predict their corresponding behavioral outcomes. Grounding sufficiency predicts hallucination resistance, guardrail coverage predicts manipulation resistance, instruction consistency predicts instruction following, and tool-schema quality predicts tool use. These findings establish context measurement as a validated preflight signal for agent reliability and position context engineering as an auditable layer of agent evaluation and governance.

AI.tldr: The paper focuses on measuring context engineering as a leading indicator of AI agent reliability using a framework called ProofAgent-Harness.
AI.motivation: To address the lack of reliable measurements for context engineering, which significantly impacts AI agent behavior and reliability.
AI.method: Developed ProofAgent-Harness, an open-source tool that evaluates context quality across seven criteria, with validation performed through controlled studies.
AI.result: Findings demonstrate that context quality predicts specific behavioral outcomes, such as hallucination resistance, instruction following, and manipulation resistance.
AI.conclusion: Context measurement is a validated preflight indicator of agent reliability, and context engineering can serve as an auditable governance layer for AI agents.

title: Semantic Register Compression in Multi-Agent LLM Cascades
summary: Multi-agent LLM systems commonly decompose complex tasks into specialized roles. However, this modularity introduces a representational risk: when intermediate agents transform text across linguistic registers, they can systematically compress the semantic distinctions needed for accurate downstream decisions. We term this phenomenon semantic register compression and characterize it as an observable failure mode in multi-agent cascades. Using a three-agent pipeline (Collector-Evaluator-Decider), we quantify compression via inter-label separation in sentence-transformer embedding space. Across political fact-checking (LIAR), sentiment analysis (SST-5), and medical triage (Triagegeist), critical evaluation consistently reduces label separability by 41.7% at the Evaluator stage, while identity passthrough preserves it nearly fully. Five architectural variants causally isolate oriented semantic transformation as the primary driver. A credibility-seeking variant produces minimal geometric compression yet shifts outputs toward mostly-true, demonstrating that transformation valence controls the direction of distributional collapse independently of compression magnitude. Compression generalizes across the three domains with varying intensity: 41.7% in fact-checking, 27.2% in sentiment, and 20.0% in triage. Prompt-level regression explains 78% of the variance, with operational constraints associated with lower compression. These results demonstrate that semantic register compression is a measurable and generalizable phenomenon in multi-agent LLM systems, with implications for safety evaluation in high-stakes domains.

AI.tldr: This paper identifies semantic register compression as a key issue in multi-agent large language model (LLM) pipelines, where intermediate text processing reduces the separability of semantic labels, hindering downstream decision-making.
AI.motivation: To address the safety and effectiveness concerns in multi-agent LLM systems, particularly in high-stakes domains where accurate semantic interpretation is crucial for decision-making.
AI.method: The study uses a three-agent pipeline (Collector-Evaluator-Decider) and measures semantic compression via inter-label separation in sentence-transformer embedding space across three domains: political fact-checking, sentiment analysis, and medical triage.
AI.result: Semantic evaluation reduces label separability by up to 41.7% in political fact-checking, with varying compression intensity across sentiment analysis (27.2%) and medical triage (20.0%). Variants of architectures reveal causal drivers and valence effects.
AI.conclusion: Semantic register compression is a systematic failure mode in multi-agent systems that affects label separability and leads to potential misjudgments, which is critical for improving system safety in sensitive domains like healthcare and politics.

title: Representation-Aligned Tactile Grounding for Contact-Rich Robotic Manipulation
summary: Tactile-enhanced vision-language-action (VLA) policies have been introduced for contact-rich manipulation, where critical interaction states are often hidden from vision. Future tactile prediction is a promising way to use touch because it turns tactile outcomes into supervision for action-induced contact dynamics. Yet VLA policies contain representations with different roles, from perceptual encoding to motor prediction, making it unclear where this supervision should be applied. We study this as a representation-alignment problem. Through a linear probe analysis, we find that future tactile states are most predictable from intermediate action-expert features, rather than from vision-language features or final action states. Motivated by this observation, we introduce a lightweight Latent Tactile Predictor (LTP), which predicts compact future tactile embeddings from the identified intermediate representation. By avoiding direct prediction of noisy raw tactile signals, LTP provides an action-outcome grounding signal that aligns intermediate action representations with future contact consequences. Experiments on real-world contact-rich manipulation tasks show that representation-aligned tactile grounding outperforms less aligned or multi-interface tactile prediction, highlighting the importance of where tactile supervision is applied.

AI.tldr: The study introduces a Latent Tactile Predictor (LTP) to enhance tactile-grounded vision-language-action (VLA) policies by predicting future tactile outcomes from intermediate representations, outperforming other methods in contact-rich manipulation tasks.
AI.motivation: The paper addresses the challenge of leveraging tactile sensing in contact-rich manipulation tasks, where visual input is often insufficient to capture critical interaction states.
AI.method: The study identifies the optimal alignment point for tactile supervision within VLA policies and proposes a Latent Tactile Predictor (LTP) to predict compact tactile embeddings from intermediate action-expert features.
AI.result: Experiments demonstrate that LTP, which provides representation-aligned tactile grounding, enhances manipulation performance compared to less aligned or multi-interface tactile prediction methods.
AI.conclusion: Strategically aligning tactile supervision within representation layers improves tactile-grounded VLA policy effectiveness, underlining the importance of targeted supervision for robust manipulation.

title: Compression of 3D Gaussian Splatting Data Using GPU-friendly Graphics Texture Coding
summary: Techniques for modeling 3D scenes from image collections, such as 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS), are capable of generating high-quality novel views by leveraging graphics primitives with view-dependent appearance. In 3DGS, spherical harmonic (SH) are employed to model view-dependent color, resulting in a large number of SH coefficients per primitive and large memory requirements. While compression approaches have been proposed to mitigate this problem, they do not exploit the capabilities of modern Graphics Processing Units (GPUs) for parallel decoding and rendering. In this paper, we propose a method for compressing SH color coefficients using texture compression schemes specifically designed for efficient parallel GPU decoding and supported by dedicated hardware acceleration. It is shown that those methods can compress color coefficients more effectively than 2D textures by exploiting the fact that primitives can be locally grouped and reordered according to color. Furthermore, we introduce a bit-rate control strategy that preserves random access, enabling large-scale parallelization without compromising rendering performance. Experimental results using BC1 and BC7 texture compression formats show that GPU-based decompression can be achieved with negligible or imperceptible degradation in the visual quality of rendered 3DGS scenes.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a method for compressing spherical harmonic color coefficients in 3D Gaussian Splatting using GPU-based texture compression, enabling efficient parallel decoding and rendering with negligible visual degradation.
AI.motivation: The high memory requirements of spherical harmonic coefficients in 3D Gaussian Splatting limit performance and scalability, necessitating effective compression methods integrated with GPU capabilities.
AI.method: The paper develops a texture compression scheme that leverages GPU hardware for parallel decoding and rendering by grouping and reordering primitives locally based on color. A bit-rate control strategy ensures efficient parallelization and random access.
AI.result: Experimental results demonstrate that GPU-based decompression using BC1 and BC7 texture formats achieves effective compression while maintaining high visual quality in 3DGS renderings.
AI.conclusion: The proposed GPU-friendly compression method significantly reduces memory demands, enables large-scale parallelization, and delivers high-quality rendering performance in 3D Gaussian Splatting systems.

title: Delocalization of bias in unadjusted Hamiltonian Monte Carlo and underdamped Langevin
summary: Unadjusted samplers such as unadjusted Hamiltonian Monte Carlo and underdamped Langevin are well-known to be biased. Metropolis--Hastings adjustment has been conventionally incorporated into Hamiltonian Monte Carlo to eliminate the bias. However, this adjustment can significantly increase the iteration complexity due to the small step size required for reasonable Metropolis acceptance rates. In this work, we extend the \emph{delocalization of bias} phenomenon, previously established for the overdamped Langevin algorithm, to these two unadjusted algorithms. We show that to control the $W_2$ bias of any $K$-dimensional marginal of a high-dimensional distribution, $O(\sqrt{K})$ integration steps suffice up to $\log d$ terms, assuming either weak or sparse interactions among variables. The discrete-time integrators here introduce technical difficulties beyond those of the overdamped setting, which we address through a broadly applicable matrix-polynomial framework that characterizes their propagators. Our result for the underdamped Langevin algorithm is valid for all large friction parameters, implying that the Leimkuhler-Matthews integrator for the overdamped Langevin dynamics also exhibits delocalization of bias.

AI.tldr: This paper studies bias in unadjusted samplers like Hamiltonian Monte Carlo and underdamped Langevin, showing that delocalization of bias holds for these algorithms under certain conditions.
AI.motivation: To address biases in unadjusted samplers and reduce the inefficiencies introduced by the conventional Metropolis--Hastings adjustment.
AI.method: The authors extend the theory of delocalization of bias using a matrix-polynomial framework to handle discrete-time integrators, investigating $W_2$ biases in high-dimensional distributions under weak or sparse variable interactions.
AI.result: They demonstrate that $O(\sqrt{K})$ integration steps suffice to control $W_2$ bias in $K$-dimensional marginals, and extend the delocalization phenomenon to underdamped Langevin algorithms with large friction parameters.
AI.conclusion: Their findings provide theoretical insights into efficient bias control for unadjusted samplers, and validate the delocalization phenomenon across different sampler settings.

title: NeuroGRIP: Retrieval-Augmented Graph Refinement for Knowledge-Grounded EEG Seizure Diagnosis
summary: Seizure diagnosis from EEG signals is a critical yet persistently challenging task, due to the complicated neural dynamics and the spurious connections in inter-channel modeling. While spatial-temporal graph neural networks (STGNNs) have advanced EEG brain network representation learning, the resulting graph structures suffer from low clinical plausibility and limited interpretability due to their purely data-driven nature. To this end, we introduce NeuroGRIP, a retrieval-augmented graph refinement framework that incorporates external medical knowledge to calibrate noisy EEG graphs. We first construct a large-scale, domain-specific knowledge base derived from authoritative clinical guidelines. Leveraging large language models, we extract structured biomedical entities and relations to form a textual knowledge graph (KG), which serves as external knowledge source of clinical priors. Our framework performs alignment-aware query construction by projecting STGNN-generated EEG node embeddings into the semantic space of KG. Semantic queries are then executed via FAISS-based similarity search over knowledge triplets to retrieve relation evidence. Each predicted edge is assigned a confidence score based on retrieved similarity, relation type, and source reliability, enabling us to prune medically implausible edges from the originally predicted graph. Extensive experiments on TUSZ and CHB-MIT demonstrate that NeuroGRIP not only improves seizure detection accuracy but also enhances interpretability by grounding each prediction in clinically validated knowledge. This work provides the first unified framework that tightly couples brain dynamics with external medical expertise via retrieval-augmented reasoning, paving the way for knowledge-enhanced, explainable clinical diagnosis. The code is available at: https://github.com/LincanLi-X/NeuroGRIP.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces NeuroGRIP, a graph refinement framework that uses external medical knowledge to enhance seizure detection and graph interpretability in EEG data.
AI.motivation: To overcome challenges in seizure diagnosis from EEG signals caused by complex neural dynamics and the lack of clinical plausibility in graph-based spatial-temporal EEG analysis.
AI.method: The framework constructs a textual knowledge graph derived from medical guidelines and integrates it with spatial-temporal graphs of EEG data. It refines the graph structure using retrieval-augmented reasoning with similarity searches to enhance medical realism.
AI.result: The framework improves seizure detection accuracy and interpretability by validating predictions through external clinical knowledge. Experiments demonstrate its effectiveness on TUSZ and CHB-MIT datasets.
AI.conclusion: NeuroGRIP bridges brain dynamics with external medical knowledge, offering a unified framework for knowledge-driven, explainable, and clinically validated EEG analysis.

title: StructureClaw: Traceable LLM Agents and an Executable Benchmark for Structural Engineering Workflows
summary: Addressing a structural-engineering request requires more than a single answer; it requires a chain of interdependent artifacts: interpreted requirements, a computable model, validation records, solver outputs, code-check records, and a final report. Evaluations centered on question answering or script generation rarely verify this complete evidence chain and may therefore reward fluent outputs even when the underlying engineering workflow is incomplete, internally inconsistent, or non-executable. To address this limitation, we present StructureClaw, an artifact-centered workbench in which LLM agents operate through governed engineering skills, typed tools, shared artifact state, and local analysis backends. We also introduce StructureClaw-Bench, an executable benchmark of 150 controlled scenarios spanning standard workflow execution, interactive robustness, and multimodal structural-model reconstruction. A scenario succeeds only when all required artifact- and execution-level assertions pass in a single run. Across ten agent-model configurations, each evaluated on the same 50 standard cases, the average Success Rate rises from 56.8% with the generic-skill baseline to 88.6% with the full automatic workflow. The interactive and multimodal evaluations identify two prominent remaining challenges: safe handling of invalid numerical inputs and fixture-consistent reconstruction of structural models. These findings show that artifact-centered evaluation can expose workflow-level failures that are difficult to identify from final responses alone, providing a more rigorous basis for evaluating and improving structural-engineering agents. The code and benchmark are available at https://github.com/structureclaw/structureclaw.

AI.tldr: This paper presents StructureClaw, a framework that evaluates LLM agents in structural engineering workflows rather than just final outputs, ensuring consistency across interdependent artifacts.
AI.motivation: Current evaluations of engineering AI agents focus on output fluency rather than ensuring complete, valid, and executable workflows, which risks overlooking significant inconsistencies or errors.
AI.method: Introduced StructureClaw, a workbench where LLM agents manage workflows via defined skills, tools, shared state, and local analysis, along with StructureClaw-Bench, an executable benchmark simulating 150 controlled engineering scenarios.
AI.result: The full automatic workflow achieved an 88.6% success rate on standard cases, while baselines had significantly lower results. Key challenges remain in handling invalid numerical inputs and consistent structural model reconstruction.
AI.conclusion: Artifact-centered evaluations, like those in StructureClaw, reveal workflow-level issues and offer a reliable way to evaluate and improve AI models for structural engineering tasks.

title: Measuring How Students Rely on Generative AI in Academic Writing: Development and Multi-Source Validation of the Generative AI Reliance Types Scale (GenAI-RTS)
summary: As generative AI (GenAI) becomes increasingly embedded in undergraduate academic writing, how students rely on these tools, rather than simply whether they use them, has become a central question for learning, academic integrity, and educational equity. Existing measures of reliance were developed inductively, focused on discrete problem-solving tasks, and validated mainly with homogeneous samples. This study developed and validated the GenAI Reliance Types Scale (GenAI-RTS), a 20-item instrument measuring four theoretically derived types of GenAI reliance: Strategic, Instrumental, Dependent, and Dialogic. Validation followed the multisource framework of the Standards for Educational and Psychological Testing, drawing on a survey of 382 undergraduates at a U.S. Minority-Serving Institution and interviews with 14 purposively sampled students. Confirmatory factor analyses of six competing models supported a five-factor structure in which Strategic Reliance comprises two facets, Deliberate Use and Critical Evaluation, alongside Instrumental, Dependent, and Dialogic factors (CFI = .92, RMSEA = .08; DWLS CFI = .98, RMSEA = .07). Subscale reliability was acceptable to good (omega = .75-.88), and scalar measurement invariance held across gender, first-generation status, and STEM/non-STEM majors, to our knowledge the first such evidence for a GenAI reliance instrument. Rasch analysis indicated that a five-point response format would improve category functioning. Strategic reliance was positively associated with AI literacy, and the reliance types differentiated students across multiple writing process and outcome variables. The GenAI-RTS offers researchers and educators a theoretically grounded, psychometrically validated instrument for identifying undergraduate reliance profiles and supporting research, assessment, and AI literacy intervention.

AI.tldr: The study develops and validates the 20-item GenAI Reliance Types Scale (GenAI-RTS) to measure four types of reliance on generative AI: Strategic, Instrumental, Dependent, and Dialogic.
AI.motivation: To understand how students rely on generative AI tools, ensuring better educational outcomes, academic integrity, and equity in learning, addressing a gap in previous reliance measurement methods.
AI.method: Developed a theoretically derived scale using surveys and interviews with undergraduate students, employing confirmatory factor analyses, Rasch analysis, and scalar measurement invariance for validation.
AI.result: The GenAI-RTS showed a five-factor structure with good subscale reliability, distinguished reliance types, and offered evidence of its applicability across various demographic groups.
AI.conclusion: The GenAI-RTS is a valid tool for assessing generative AI reliance in undergraduate students, aiding research, assessments, and literacy interventions in educational contexts.

title: Cross-Dataset Generalization in Urdu Fake News Detection: An Empirical Study with XLM-RoBERTa and a Length Confound Analysis
comment: 10 pages, 7 figures, 5 tables, submitted to arXiv
summary: Urdu fake news detection remains under-resourced despite Urdu being spoken by over 231 million people worldwide. While prior work has demonstrated strong in-domain performance on individual Urdu datasets, cross-dataset generalisation has received little systematic attention. This paper presents the first cross-dataset generalisation study for Urdu fake news detection, using two publicly available balanced datasets: the Ax-to-Grind Urdu corpus (10,083 articles, 15 domains) and the Notri-Fact Urdu dataset (13,388 articles). We fine-tune xlm-roberta-base under four experimental conditions, in-domain on each dataset and two zero-shot cross-domain transfer directions, comparing against TF-IDF baselines using Logistic Regression and Support Vector Machines. Our experiments reveal a striking asymmetry: Notri-Fact to Ax-to-Grind transfer achieves a macro F1 of 0.771, while the reverse collapses to F1 of 0.005, with the model predicting fake for 99.7% of test articles. We demonstrate that this collapse stems from a systematic length confound in Ax-to-Grind, where fake articles average 117 words versus 35 for real articles, a 3.4x asymmetry inducing shortcut learning. A length ablation capping articles at 50 words yields only a 0.0067 F1 drop, confirming the confound inflates but does not solely drive in-domain performance. We provide a reusable diagnostic methodology that combines bidirectional transfer analysis and prediction-collapse inspection to identify confound-driven behavior in multilingual fake news detection settings.

AI.tldr: The study investigates cross-dataset generalization for Urdu fake news detection and identifies biases in datasets that affect performance.
AI.motivation: To address the lack of research in cross-dataset generalization for Urdu fake news detection and to identify systematic issues in existing datasets.
AI.method: The authors fine-tuned xlm-roberta-base for in-domain and cross-domain experiments on two datasets, comparing it with baseline models and analyzing confounders through length ablation and bidirectional transfer analysis.
AI.result: The study revealed a severe asymmetry in cross-dataset performance, rooted in a length confound within one dataset that caused shortcut learning for fake news detection.
AI.conclusion: The research highlights the need for careful dataset evaluation to avoid confounds in fake news detection and provides reusable methodologies for analyzing such biases in multilingual contexts.

title: Image-to-Point Cloud Registration Made Easy with Rectified Flow-based LiDAR Upsampling
summary: Image-to-Point Cloud Registration (I2P) is essential for integrating camera and LiDAR in perception and autonomous systems, yet the modality gap between images and point clouds makes it difficult to achieve both high accuracy and strong generalization. In this paper, we propose a simple yet effective I2P method that treats LiDAR as an imaging sensor: from a single sparse LiDAR scan, we generate a dense LiDAR intensity image using Conditional Rectified Flow, match it with a camera image using a pre-trained feature matcher, and estimate the 6-DoF relative pose via PnP-RANSAC. The proposed model is pre-trained through a self-supervised image completion task and fine-tuned on a small amount of LiDAR data (neither image-point cloud pairs nor ground-truth sensor poses are required), enabling it to scale to diverse LiDAR and camera configurations. Experiments on the R3LIVE dataset show that the proposed method achieves a mean error of 4.89° / 1.63 m, outperforming existing methods, while completing a single registration in approximately 0.68 s.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a method for integrating camera and LiDAR data using image-to-point cloud registration, improving accuracy and generalization despite modality gaps.
AI.motivation: Bridging the modality gap between images and point clouds for improved integration of camera and LiDAR data in autonomous systems.
AI.method: Using Conditional Rectified Flow to create dense LiDAR images from sparse scans, matching them to camera images via pre-trained feature matchers, and estimating pose with PnP-RANSAC.
AI.result: Achieves state-of-the-art performance with a mean error of 4.89° / 1.63 m, completing registration in approximately 0.68 seconds on the R3LIVE dataset.
AI.conclusion: The method is scalable across camera and LiDAR setups, leveraging self-supervised learning and minimal LiDAR data while outperforming existing approaches.

title: VTM-Nav: Hierarchical Visual-Topological Memory for Cross-Episode Object-Goal Navigation
summary: Object-goal navigation requires an embodied agent to locate and reach an instance of a specified object category in an indoor environment. Recent training-free approaches leverage vision-language models (VLMs) for open-vocabulary semantic reasoning, but are typically evaluated under an episodic protocol that resets all scene-specific state after each episode. We introduce Cross-Episode Object-Goal Navigation, in which an agent repeatedly operates in the same scene, retains only self-acquired experience, and keeps its model parameters fixed. To support experience reuse, we present \method, a training-free VLM navigation framework with a persistent hierarchical Visual-Topological Memory (VTM). The VTM organizes scene knowledge at room and object levels and retrieves relevant experience through coarse-to-fine matching, providing memory as soft guidance only when it agrees with current observations. A conservative execution guard further mitigates oscillations, blocked motions, and premature stopping. Under a controlled same-scene protocol, we evaluate \method{} on three benchmarks, HM3D v0.1, HM3D v0.2, and MP3D, and compare it with a strengthened WMNav baseline augmented with cross-episode textual memory, while keeping the VLM backbone and action pipeline identical. \method{} achieves the best performance across all three benchmarks, demonstrating the effectiveness and robustness of structured visual-topological experience reuse across datasets.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces Cross-Episode Object-Goal Navigation, where an agent navigates repeatedly in the same scene using self-acquired experience without modifying its model. The proposed framework, method, utilizes a Visual-Topological Memory (VTM) for hierarchical memory reuse and achieves state-of-the-art performance across benchmarks.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to improve object-goal navigation by enabling agents to leverage self-acquired experience in repeated interactions within the same scene, overcoming limitations of episodic protocols.
AI.method: The method introduces a Visual-Topological Memory (VTM) for structured reuse of scene knowledge, utilizing coarse-to-fine matching and conservative execution strategies. The approach is training-free and relies on vision-language models (VLMs).
AI.result: The framework significantly outperformed the baseline (WMNav) across three benchmarks (HM3D v0.1, HM3D v0.2, MP3D), showcasing its robustness and effectiveness in experience reuse.
AI.conclusion: method provides a robust, training-free solution for object-goal navigation by effectively using hierarchical scene memory, establishing state-of-the-art results across benchmarks.

title: Data Driven Block Replacement Scheduling
summary: We develop data-driven algorithms for maintaining $N$ independent identical machines under a \textit{block replacement policy}, in which each machine is replaced upon failure and all machines are jointly replaced at regular intervals of length $k$. The goal is to learn the cost-minimizing interval $k^*$ from operational data when the lifetime distribution is unknown. At each decision epoch, the operator selects $k \in \{1, 2, \ldots, K\}$, observes the resulting failure history (a mixture of complete and right-censored lifetimes) and incurs a per-unit-time cost governed by the renewal function. We formulate this as a stochastic multi-armed bandit and propose Hoeffding- and Bernstein-based lower-confidence-bound algorithms achieving $O(K \log T)$ regret, matching the Lai--Robbins lower bound. Exploiting a nested observation property unique to block replacement, correlated variants attain $O((K-k^*)\log T)$ regret and require only $O(1)$ direct pulls of suboptimal arms $k < k^*$. A complementary Kaplan--Meier renewal algorithm estimates the lifetime distribution nonparametrically from censored data, achieving almost-sure policy consistency and empirically near-zero incremental regret at long horizons. We additionally analyze two average-cost MDPs: a time-elapsed formulation establishing that block replacement is optimal within its policy class for any lifetime distribution, and an age-vector formulation proving a monotone threshold structure under increasing failure rate distributions and providing a gold-standard cost benchmark. Numerical experiments confirm the theoretical ordering and reveal structural cost gaps between optimal block and age-dependent replacement.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes algorithms for optimizing maintenance schedules of machines under a block replacement policy by learning the ideal replacement interval using operational data, achieving theoretical efficiency and practical insights.
AI.motivation: To determine cost-efficient maintenance intervals for machines in scenarios where the lifetime distribution of the machines is unknown, addressing challenges in decision-making under uncertainty.
AI.method: The study formulates the block replacement problem as a stochastic multi-armed bandit problem. It develops lower-confidence-bound algorithms with $O(K \log T)$ regret, introduces correlated variants with $O((K-k^*)\log T)$ regret, and applies a Kaplan-Meier renewal algorithm for estimating lifetime distributions non-parametrically.
AI.result: The proposed algorithms achieve theoretical performance bounds (low regret), policy consistency, and effective cost minimization compared to gold-standard benchmarks. Numerical experiments validate theoretical predictions and demonstrate structural cost differentials.
AI.conclusion: The results confirm the proposed algorithms effectively learn optimal maintenance schedules and demonstrate the competitive advantage and limitations of block replacement policies over alternative maintenance strategies.

title: Towards a Unified Multidimensional Explainability Metric: Evaluating Trustworthiness in AI Models
summary: In this paper, we present a comprehensive framework for assessing the explainability of various XAI methods, such as LIME and SHAP, across multiple datasets and machine learning models, with the ultimate goal of creating a unified multidimensional explainability score. Our methodology focuses on three key aspects of explainability: fidelity, simplicity, and stability. We leverage benchmarking experiments to systematically evaluate these aspects and use the insights gained to construct an offline knowledge base. This knowledge base captures the explainability scores for each registered model and serves as a valuable resource for context-dependent evaluation of explainability. By analyzing the complementary characteristics and metadata of AI models, datasets, and XAI methods, the knowledge base will enable the estimation of explainability scores for previously unseen datasets and models. Properties like fidelity, simplicity, and stability may vary significantly based on the dataset, underlying model, and domain expertise of the end user. We demonstrate our framework by applying it to three open-source datasets, discussing the implications of the obtained results in relation to the characteristics of the datasets. Our work contributes to the growing field of XAI by providing a robust and versatile tool for evaluating and comparing the explainability of various XAI methods, ultimately supporting the development of more transparent and trustworthy AI systems.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a framework for evaluating the explainability of XAI methods (e.g., LIME, SHAP) across datasets and models, aimed at creating a unified explainability score.
AI.motivation: The authors aim to address the lack of standardized evaluation metrics for XAI by developing a unified multidimensional explainability score.
AI.method: The framework assesses explainability based on fidelity, simplicity, and stability using benchmarking experiments, creating a knowledge base for future evaluations.
AI.result: The framework enables estimation of explainability scores for unseen datasets and models, showcasing its applicability via experiments on three open-source datasets.
AI.conclusion: This work contributes to XAI by offering a robust tool for evaluating explainability, promoting AI transparency and trustworthiness.

title: FirmPilot: Evidence-Guided Multi-Agent Environment Recovery for IoT Firmware Rehosting
summary: Firmware rehosting executes firmware images in emulated environments such as QEMU to enable scalable dynamic analysis of Internet of Things (IoT) devices. In practice, rehosting pipelines remain fragile across diverse real-world firmware images, as reaching an externally observable execution state depends on tightly coupled artifacts spanning boot scripts, persistent configuration (e.g., NVRAM-like key-value state), and network setup. Template-driven frameworks often fail to accommodate long-tail vendor conventions, while unconstrained use of large language models (LLMs) risks unsupported modifications and irreproducible executions. We introduce FirmPilot, an evidence-guided multi-agent framework for environment recovery in firmware rehosting. FirmPilot reformulates rehosting as iterative environment reconstruction in which a search agent grounds decisions through similarity-based retrieval, a planner coordinates execution-accepted transitions, and specialized agents recover filesystem/init artifacts, persistent state, and network exposure. Through repeated execution and evidence-grounded artifact deltas, the system resolves cross-layer dependencies across boot, state, and networking that otherwise prevent firmware executions from reaching a stable, externally reachable state in emulation. Evaluated on the large-scale, real-world LFwC firmware corpus, FirmPilot improves web-service reachability over FirmAE from 25.49% to 52.39% and network reachability from 39.30% to 71.93%. The resulting rehosts raise the average number of detected services per firmware from 0.86 to 1.62 and support downstream analysis workflows, including RouterSploit interaction and protocol-aware fuzzing over recovered service surfaces. The evaluation shows that evidence- and feedback-grounded agent coordination improves rehosting success, service recovery, and downstream utility in automated firmware rehosting.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces FirmPilot, a framework that improves firmware rehosting by resolving environment recovery challenges. It significantly enhances reachability and service detection for IoT firmware in emulated environments.
AI.motivation: The need to overcome the fragility of existing firmware rehosting pipelines, which struggle with diverse real-world firmware and fail to reliably reach observable execution states.
AI.method: FirmPilot employs a multi-agent framework where agents iteratively reconstruct execution environments using evidence-guided retrieval, planning, and artifact recovery processes across boot, state, and networking layers.
AI.result: FirmPilot markedly improves web-service reachability (from 25.49% to 52.39%), network reachability (from 39.30% to 71.93%), and the average number of services detected per firmware, outperforming the baseline comparison (FirmAE).
AI.conclusion: The evidence-based, feedback-guided agent coordination in FirmPilot substantially enhances firmware rehosting, enabling more effective downstream analysis and interaction opportunities for IoT firmware dynamic analysis.

title: Tracing LLM Behavior to the Training Data with Empirical Next-Token Distributions
summary: In this paper, we study the connection between an LLM's output distribution and the data used to train it. Specifically, we study the degree to which an LLM's next-token distribution agrees with the empirical next-token distribution (ENTD) given the context in the training data. The ENTD is an appealing target because it is the unrestricted global minimizer of the next-token cross entropy loss used for pretraining, as well as an easily interpretable function of the pretraining corpus. We find that for a significant fraction of inputs, the LLM's distribution agrees with the ENTD almost perfectly, and the average agreement increases with model scale and training compute. Nevertheless, there is a long tail of input sequences where the LLM and ENTD differ significantly, and we examine several possible sources of this discrepancy across the transformer architecture, training procedure, and finite-sample noise in the ENTD estimate itself. More broadly, we hope our findings will encourage more work on ``data-centric mechanistic interpretability,'' a complement to standard mechanistic interpretability that opens the black box of how model behaviors arise from the data, rather than how they are encoded in the learned weights.

AI.tldr: This paper investigates the connection between an LLM's output distribution and the training data's empirical next-token distribution (ENTD). It finds close agreement for many contexts, with discrepancies in specific cases attributed to multiple factors.
AI.motivation: The authors aim to understand how the next-token predictions of an LLM are influenced by the empirical next-token distribution derived from the training data, an understudied aspect of LLM behavior.
AI.method: They compare LLM's next-token distribution with the empirical next-token distribution (ENTD) across various inputs and analyze patterns of agreement and divergence. Factors such as model scale, training compute, architecture, procedure, and finite-sample noise are explored.
AI.result: A significant fraction of inputs show near-perfect agreement between the LLM and ENTD, and agreement tends to increase with larger model scales and training compute. However, certain inputs exhibit substantial divergences.
AI.conclusion: The findings highlight the potential for understanding model behavior in terms of training data characteristics, encouraging work on "data-centric mechanistic interpretability."

title: Breaking Refusal in the First Half: A Mechanistic Study of the Prefill Jailbreak
comment: 31 pages, 3 figures. Code and derived artifacts: https://github.com/collapseindex/breaking-refusal
summary: Aligned language models refuse harmful requests, but a one-line prefill ("Sure, here is") strips the refusal. We ask where and how it fails. The harm representation stays intact: on the prompts the attack flips to compliance, a linear probe reads harm as high as on the refused ones (0.91-0.98), while behavioral refusal drops to chance. This holds across four models and three families (1.5-3.8B, and at 14B). Refusal is therefore a shallow, response-site computation. We localize it to an early window: a dose-matched position control shows the first half of the response suffices to break refusal, while the second half is nearly inert. Three causal probes converge on that window. Restoring the harm direction there partially re-engages refusal. Injecting the model's own refuse-state reverses the jailbreak (74%, held-out). And knocking out the early response's attention to the prefill, but not an equal attention mass elsewhere, selectively collapses the harmful continuation. A base-model control identifies the mechanism: the same knockout collapses the continuation prefill-specifically even in a non-safety-tuned base model (64% to 25% harmful content vs a matched control's 64%, replicated at 7B). So the prefill's grip is generic autoregressive conditioning, not safety-specific suppression, and "refusal restoration" is a model-dependent fallback. The dominant mechanism is passive. A small safety-specific attractor remains on top (logit-trace concentration 0.24 vs 0.03), whose active-vs-passive character we size but do not fully separate. No single direction or component is a clean handle either: the decision is decodable but distributed, and refusal tracks harm rather than scary surface. The consequence is structural: a monitor reading the untouched prompt-side representation is immune by construction, but only to response-site attacks. The mechanism is diffuse; the failure surface is local.

AI.tldr: A critical analysis of aligned language models demonstrates their vulnerability to a single-line 'jailbreak' that bypasses refusal mechanisms to harmful prompts. The study identifies refusal as a shallow and localized phenomenon while understanding the structural vulnerabilities of these safety mechanisms.
AI.motivation: To investigate how and why aligned language models' safety mechanisms fail when provided specific 'jailbreak' inputs and explore whether these failures are rooted in their design.
AI.method: The authors use experimental probes, causal analysis, and control tests across multiple models and sizes (1.5B-14B parameters), focusing on where and how refusal mechanisms break under a 'prefill' setup. They also examine the mechanisms involved by altering specific parts of the model's internal state and response sequence.
